article_title: Historical evolution of microvascular decompression after Jannetta’s establishment: Anatomical maps and physiological compasses—a narrative review
label: Fig. 3
caption: “Rule of Three” concept and individualized approaches of MVD. The “Rule of Three” concept provided one practical framework for three tailored approaches for the three major neurovascular compression syndromes, i.e., trigeminal neuralgia (TN), hemifacial spasm (HFS), and glossopharyngeal neuralgia (GPN): (Left) the lateral supracerebellar-infratentorial route through the tentorial surface for TN, most commonly associated with SCA compression; (Center) the infrafloccular route through the petrosal surface for HFS, commonly associated with AICA compression; and (Right) the optional transcondylar fossa route through the suboccipital surface for GPN, most commonly associated with PICA compression. The illustration also accurately depicts the characteristic upward PICA loop wedged between the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves in GPN. Modified with permission from Hitotsumatsu et al. [23]

article_title: Historical evolution of microvascular decompression after Jannetta’s establishment: Anatomical maps and physiological compasses—a narrative review
label: Fig. 5
caption: Noncompressive transposition technique. Following Jannetta’s establishment of MVD, interposition was widely practiced; however, long-term outcomes reported from the late 1980s onward prompted wider evaluation and refinement of noncompressive transposition. Fukushima had illustrated this concept as early as 1982 in a pioneering figure, and subsequent technique-focused series demonstrated more durable relief with pure noncompressive transposition than with interposition. Notably, that report also depicted tailored craniotomy windows, at a time when a uniform large lateral suboccipital craniotomy was commonly used irrespective of diagnosis. Modified with permission from Fukushima [16]

article_title: Microsurgical Management of Fourth Ventricle Lesions Via the Median Suboccipital Keyhole Telovelar Approach
label: FIGURE 1
caption: Midline suboccipital keyhole craniotomy for lesions of the fourth ventricle. (A) The skin incision was placed median extending from the line between the tips of the bilateral mastoid to 3 cm upwards and 1 cm downwards. (B) An osteoplastic craniotomy with a diameter of nearly 2.0 cm was performed using a 2-mm high-speed craniotome. (C) The bilateral cerebellar tonsils, uvula, posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA), foramen of Magendie, and tela choroidea could be observed after opening and retraction of the arachnoid.

article_title: Microsurgical Management of Fourth Ventricle Lesions Via the Median Suboccipital Keyhole Telovelar Approach
label: FIGURE 2
caption: Preoperative and postoperative enhanced magnetic resonance imaging in a typical case. (A–C) Preoperative magnetic resonance imaging revealed a mass in the fourth ventricle with obstructive hydrocephalus. (D–F) Postoperative magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated successful gross total resection of the tumor.

article_title: Microsurgical Management of Fourth Ventricle Lesions Via the Median Suboccipital Keyhole Telovelar Approach
label: FIGURE 3
caption: Intraoperative photos of a typical case. (A) The bilateral cerebellar tonsils, uvula, and posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) could be observed after opening and retraction of the arachnoid. (B) By visualizing the inferior part of the tumor, its protrusion through the foramen of Magendie was evident, with the tela choroidea and inferior medullary velum stretched thinly over its surface. (C) After the retraction of the vermis and the inferior medullary velum, the entire tumor was exposed and totally resected piece by piece. (D) The aqueduct was inspected and its patency was verified at the end of the resection.

article_title: Medial-tonsillar telovelar approach for resection of a superior medullary velum cerebral cavernous malformation: anatomical and tractography study of the surgical approach and functional implications
label: Fig. 1
caption: Main anatomic external landmarks of brainstem and cerebellum: a Midline sagittal view of the brainstem and cerebellum; b coronal view at the level of the middle cerebellar peduncles of the floor of the fourth ventricle; c view of the suboccipital surface of the cerebellum (P, Pyramid; T, Tonsil) showing the natural corridor used in the unilateral uvulotonsillar approach (red arrow)

article_title: Medial-tonsillar telovelar approach for resection of a superior medullary velum cerebral cavernous malformation: anatomical and tractography study of the surgical approach and functional implications
label: Fig. 2
caption: Anatomy of the microsurgical approach to the superior medullary velum via a medial-tonsillar approach: a suboccipital surface of the cerebellum and posterior aspect of the medulla oblongata; b brain retractors on top of the tonsils to expose the corridor in-between the tonsils (laterally) and the uvula and pyramid (medially) with exposure of the foramen of Magendie; c posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) trajectory from the lateral aspect of the medulla oblongata (with its relation with the IX, X, XI and XII cranial nerves) towards the cerebellar-medullary fissure and its relation with the tonsil and the uvula; d exposure of the tela choroidea, inferior medullary velum and the telovelar junction; e exposure of the floor and lateral recess of the fourth ventricle after dividing the tela choroidea and the inferior medullary velum; f exposure of the superior cerebellar peduncle above the lateral recess of the fourth ventricle

article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 2
caption: Early anatomical description of the far-lateral approach (FLA). Early anatomical and technical depictions of the FLA from the report by Heros [25], which was widely regarded as the first detailed surgical description of this technique. The approach was developed to access aneurysms of the distal vertebral artery and basilar trunk by extending the standard lateral suboccipital craniectomy to include removal of the lateral rim of the foramen magnum. A Illustration showing the preferred direction of the approach, showing the operative corridor created between the accessory nerve (XI) inferiorly and the glossopharyngeal (IX) and vagus (X) nerves superiorly, which allows access to proximal posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) aneurysms. B Illustration depicting the original description of the skin incision, bony removal, and dural opening involving the region just posterior to the occipital condyle, with resection of the lateral margin of the foramen magnum to enhance exposure. Panels A and B reproduced with permission from Heros [25]

article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 3
caption: Variations in skin incision design for the far-lateral approach. A Photograph showing a right-sided U-shaped modified far lateral incision extending from just below the mastoid tip inferiorly, sweeping posteriorly to reach the midline around the level of C2, representing a more extensive and aggressive version of the exposure. This particular incision is very helpful when an occipital artery is to be dissected for occipital to posterior inferior cerebellar artery bypass, as part of the procedure. Panel A modified with permission from Ali et al. [3]. B Photograph demonstrating an L-shaped incision that may be used to offer a midline and far-lateral exposure at the procedure. This is very helpful when a midline exposure is needed for occipital to cervical fusion or addressing a vascular or tumor lesion that requires midline suboccipital as well as far lateral exposure. C This photograph demonstrates a more tailored curvilinear incision placed just posterior and inferior to the sigmoid sinus. This incision is centered over the transverse process of C1 and is designed to afford early exposure of the V4 segment of the vertebral artery and a tailored transcondylar approach. Panel C reproduced with permission from Scoville et al. [44]

article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 4
caption: Variation in soft tissue and muscle dissection for the far-lateral approach. A Intraoperative photographs showing a layered, anatomically meticulous dissection technique, demonstrating muscles along the lateral groove being separated layer by layer to expose the suboccipital triangle. Panel A reproduced with permission from Sato et al. [43]. B Intraoperative photograph demonstrating the anatomy exposed on a targeted right-sided approach. Panel B © Department of Neurosurgery, University of Utah. Rec Cap Post Maj M, rectus capitis posterior major muscle; Sup Obl M, superior oblique muscle; Semispin Cap M, semispinalis capitis muscle; OA, occipital artery; Rec Cap Post Min M, recuts capitis posterior minor muscle; Inf, inferior; n, nerve; VA, vertebral artery
